title: Scheffler Takes Command: Masters 2026 Round 1 Recap
excerpt: Scottie Scheffler makes a statement with an opening 67. Rory McIlroy defends his title with a solid 69. The stage is set for a thrilling final three rounds at Augusta.
body:
Scheffler and McIlroy Set the Pace on Opening Day<\/h2>

The 90th Masters Tournament got underway on Thursday at Augusta National, and the stage is set for a dramatic week of golf. Scottie Scheffler, the world No. 1 and 5\/1 favourite, made an emphatic statement of intent with a first-round 67, birdying five holes to sit just two shots off the early lead. Defending champion Rory McIlroy, seeking back-to-back Green Jackets, kept himself firmly in contention with an opening 69, while Bryson DeChambeau—fresh from back-to-back LIV Golf victories last month—carded a steady 68.<\/p>

Scheffler Round 1<\/div>
67 (-5)<\/div><\/div>
McIlroy Round 1<\/div>
69 (-3)<\/div><\/div>
Early Leader<\/div>
-7<\/div><\/div>
Field Size<\/div>
96<\/div><\/div><\/div>

The Firm Hand at the Top<\/h2>

Scheffler's 67 was built on precision—particularly off the tee, where his ball-striking prowess was on full display. He reached 16 of 18 greens in regulation and converted his opportunities when they came. This is a player who has dominated the PGA Tour all season, and Augusta's demands for accurate approach play into his wheelhouse perfectly. His position of strength suggests he could very well claim his second Green Jacket before the week is done.<\/p>

McIlroy's Green Jacket Defense Alive<\/h2>

McIlroy's 69 was a composed effort. The Northern Irishman, who broke his Masters hoodoo last year with a composed final-round charge, showed none of the nerves that had plagued him in years past. Three birdies offset one bogey, keeping him at the right altitude for a push over the coming three rounds. If he can find a rhythm on the greens, he remains a genuine threat.<\/p>

The Week Ahead<\/h2>

Friday brings Round 2, with the field beginning to separate. The azaleas are in full bloom, the rough is thick, and Augusta is playing firm—conditions that reward precision and punish sloppy play. Scheffler's early lead suggests a coronation in waiting, but the Masters has a habit of surprising the favourites. Three rounds remain, and many storylines are yet to unfold.<\/p>
